significance.explanation: Men's 3000m steeplechase final awarding gold, plus qualifications for women's javelin and high jump, and men's 800m round one. One marquee final (steeplechase) balances preliminary/qual stakes; overall significance is strong but not peak.

experience.explanation: Thursday morning (9:30 AM–12:00 PM) at the Coliseum. Steeplechasers lap the oval splashing through water jumps; high jumpers arch over bars in the infield pit. Field events and distance running in daylight lack evening's electricity, but steeplechase final is visually compelling.

starPower.explanation: Steeplechase final features world-class distance runners; Yaroslava Mahuchikh (Paris high jump champion from Ukraine) may appear in quals if competing, carrying significant cultural weight. Emmanuel Wanyonyi (Paris 800m gold) may seed heats if returning.

uniqueness.explanation: Men's steeplechase final is a singular spectacle, but steeplechase is a once-per-Games event regardless. Mahuchikh's potential return and her Ukraine narrative add intrigue; overall session remains structurally routine morning quals + one medal.

experience.explanation: Evening finals (3:30–6:30 PM) at the Coliseum with peak crowd energy and overlapping medal ceremonies. Pole vaulters chasing heights, hammer throwers launching, sprinters battling semifinals—relentless finals-night electricity.

experience.explanation: Evening finals (4:15–7:20 PM) at the Coliseum with prime-time energy and overlapping medal ceremonies. Distance runners lap the oval 25 times while sprinters dash and heptathletes finish their 200m across two days of grind—varied spectacle at peak atmosphere.

scorecard.overall: Saturday's five-gold sprint and field crescendo—with defending women's 200m rivals Gabby Thomas and Julien Alfred, 400m champions, and the men's 4x100m relay—peaks with the women's heptathlon finale. experience.explanation: Sunday evening prime time at the Coliseum with eight finals and semifinals compressed into 2.5 hours. Sunset light, peristyle arch framing the homestretch, and the venue's iconic bowl amplifies the intensity of relay exchanges and medal ceremonies back-to-back.

starPower.explanation: Men's 800m final features Emmanuel Wanyonyi (Paris 2024 gold), Marco Arop, and Djamel Sedjati among the world's fastest milers at their peak. Women's high jump brings Yaroslava Mahuchikh and other elite vertical jumpers; relay finals showcase international sprint superstars.

uniqueness.explanation: Five separate victory ceremonies in one evening at the historic Coliseum—a compressed, high-density medal gauntlet. The convergence of men's 4x100m relay final with sunset and field-and-track simultaneity creates a crescendo not replicated elsewhere in the Games.

demand.explanation: Prime-time Sunday evening with five gold medals, marquee relay final, and iconic venue reaches the ceiling of Games demand. Price ceiling near $2500 reflects scarcity and prestige; expect near-instant sell-out.

experience.explanation: Monday evening finale under lights at the Coliseum with back-to-back relay explosions, a 5000m tactical epic, and field drama interspersed. The farewell to nine days of track and field on the 1984 and 1932 Games site intensifies the ceremonial weight of each finish line.

starPower.explanation: Men's 5000m final attracts elite distance runners in peak form competing for gold over 12.5 laps. Four relay finals (two 4x100m, two 4x400m) field national sprint champions and world-record-holder teams; marquee athletes anchor each leg.

demand.explanation: The finale of track and field at an iconic venue with six gold medals and relay theatrics commands top-tier demand. Monday evening prime time, symbol of closure for the athletics program, and price ceiling near $2500 indicate sell-out pressure matching the opening.

experience.explanation: Early morning start (7:30 AM) blunts the spectator energy despite two medal events. Race walk's technical, rhythmic nature rewards close attention but lacks the explosive drama of track sprint finals; the cool morning conditions benefit athletes more than the crowd.

starPower.explanation: World-class rowers in mixed pairings, but the format combines individuals whose single-sport fame is limited. Roster uncertainty and lack of pre-Games publicity on mixed doubles teams limits recognizable names.

uniqueness.explanation: Mixed doubles in coastal rowing debuts here, adding a team dimension to a new discipline. The pairing of man and woman in open-water sprints is a fresh Games format with no historical precedent.

demand.explanation: Prelim pricing ($28–$124) and mid-morning slot suggest moderate ticket competition. New mixed format draws interest but early-round timing and non-medal status keep accessibility relatively higher.

scorecard.overall: Mixed doubles racing debuts in coastal sprints during morning prelims, introducing crew synchronization to a new discipline. Preliminary stakes and morning timing offset the novelty of mixed-gender team format.

significance.explanation: Mixed double sculls gold, silver, and bronze medals are decided in final 8 eliminations. The third and final medal event of coastal rowing's Games debut carries full competitive and historical weight.

experience.explanation: Afternoon prime-time final on a beach stage with pairs racing meters from sand and spectators at the finish line. Medal-deciding climax in a novel discipline with intimate waterfront viewing and decisive moments.

starPower.explanation: Eight finalist pairs represent elite rowers in a debut mixed format, but global name recognition remains limited pre-Games. Competitive pedigree is undisputed; mainstream fame is not.

uniqueness.explanation: The first and only Games medal in mixed coastal doubles, pairing a new discipline with medal stakes and debut novelty. This session concludes coastal rowing's inaugural run with unrepeatable historical significance.

demand.explanation: Finals pricing ($93–$229) and afternoon slot drive strong ticket appetite. Medal stakes and closing-event positioning of a debut sport elevate demand above prelims, though below traditional rowing finals.

scorecard.overall: The final coastal rowing medal awarded in mixed doubles as the discipline's curtain call, with crews racing in afternoon prime time on the beach. Debut status and podium stakes outweigh modest global name recognition.

significance.explanation: Opening heats across three boat classes set the bracket but carry no medal stakes. First races of the rowing program establish the competition, but outcomes determine only advancement, not podium finishes.

experience.explanation: Afternoon heat session at Marine Stadium offers close waterfront views and calm conditions, but early-round format lacks the intensity and finish-line drama of medal races. Relaxed, accessible atmosphere for learning the sport's rhythm.

starPower.explanation: Paris medalists like Karolien Florijn and Emma Twigg appear in opening rounds, as do world-class scullers from NED, NZL, and other top programs. Early heats often feature top contenders testing the new 1500m distance.

uniqueness.explanation: Standard opening-round format at Marine Stadium. While the 1500m distance is new to the Games, this session is one of multiple preliminary rounds across the rowing program—no single iconic pairing or once-in-a-lifetime moment.

demand.explanation: Lowest price ceiling of the rowing program ($28–$124) and early-round format make this easier to book than medal sessions. Presale interest tracks solid but not competitive; ideal for rowing enthusiasts or casual fans testing the venue.

scorecard.overall: Opening heats bring Paris medalists and world-class scullers to Marine Stadium's calm waters, but early-round stakes and daytime timing keep this session accessible and lower-pressure—a good entry point to watch the shortened 1500m format debut.

significance.explanation: Semifinals in men's double sculls and pairs carry medal-adjacent stakes, while men's single sculls and women's boats remain in early rounds. Stakes are clear—lose and go to B finals—but gold is not yet on the line.

experience.explanation: Afternoon session mixing heats and semis at Marine Stadium provides good waterfront energy without peak-time intensity. Extended window (12:00 PM–2:30 PM) lets fans move between viewing areas and watch multiple boat classes transition through rounds.

starPower.explanation: Oliver Zeidler (Paris gold in men's single sculls) competes in opening heats, along with other national champions and world-level rowers. Semifinals draw medal-contending pairs and doubles crews from top programs like NED, GER, and GBR.

uniqueness.explanation: Mid-program day with mixed rounds at Marine Stadium. Semifinals are higher-stakes than heats but lack the singular focal point of a gold-medal final; one of several semifinal sessions across the rowing schedule.

demand.explanation: Same price range as ROW01 ($28–$124). Semifinal appeal boosts demand slightly over opening heats, but lack of medal racing keeps this softer than final-round sessions. Moderate competitive pressure for tickets.

scorecard.overall: Zeidler and other Paris medalists race in heats while men's double sculls semifinals deliver higher-stakes racing, but the mixed-round format and non-medal timing keep this session as a solid mid-program entry rather than a marquee draw.

significance.explanation: First rowing gold medals awarded in men's double sculls and pairs A finals. Women's single sculls quarterfinals and women's double/pair semis set up later medal races. Three-tier session combining medal-deciding races with advancement rounds.

experience.explanation: Afternoon window (12:30 PM–3:10 PM) at Marine Stadium with medal ceremonies and finish-line drama. National anthems and medal presentations add emotional weight; extended session lets fans experience multiple boat classes and celebrate winners in real time.

starPower.explanation: Men's double sculls and pairs A finals feature medal contenders from top rowing nations. Karolien Florijn's women's single sculls quarterfinal appearance brings a defending Paris champion; all boat classes field world-class crews.

uniqueness.explanation: First gold-medal session of the rowing program at the historic Marine Stadium venue adds novelty. However, double sculls and pairs are established boat classes, not new disciplines—the uniqueness comes from being the opening medal ceremony, not from the session format itself.

demand.explanation: Price ceiling nearly doubles to $229.46; gold medals and medal ceremonies drive presale demand significantly above heats and semis. First rowing medals of the Games attract dedicated fans and completionists, but not the blockbuster demand of marquee sports finals.

scorecard.overall: First rowing gold medals in men's double sculls and pairs draw medal-contending crews and medal ceremonies to Marine Stadium's finish line. Afternoon timing and mixed rounds make it engaging for fans tracking multiple boat classes, though it lacks the singular marquee status of individual championship races.

significance.explanation: Women's double sculls and pairs award all medals (A, B, C finals) in a complete medal session. Men's quad sculls and fours heats plus men's single sculls quarterfinals add advancement stakes. Women's boat finals drive high significance.

experience.explanation: Afternoon session (1:30 PM–4:00 PM) at Marine Stadium with optimal lighting and weather patterns. Medal ceremonies for women's boats followed by the power and spectacle of men's quads and fours launching—varied boat sizes keep energy dynamic.

starPower.explanation: Oliver Zeidler races in men's single sculls quarterfinals; women's double sculls and pairs fields feature Paris medalists and world-class crews. Quad sculls and fours bring elite international teams known for coordinated power.

uniqueness.explanation: Women's boats completing their medal rounds adds novelty, but double sculls and pairs are traditional boat classes. Quad sculls and fours are established events appearing in multiple sessions—this is one of several opportunities to see them across the program.

demand.explanation: Price ceiling $229.46 and women's gold medals drive strong presale interest. Afternoon timing and medal ceremonies boost appeal, but it's one of three days with similar medal-session pricing and format—demand is healthy but not uniquely competitive.

scorecard.overall: Women's double sculls and pairs crown champions while men's quad sculls and fours debut with opening heats, creating a mixed session of medal closure and program advancement. Afternoon slot and historic venue provide solid gold-medal viewing, though traditional boat classes limit uniqueness.

significance.explanation: Men's quad sculls and fours award gold medals in A and B finals. Women's single sculls semis push Florijn and Twigg toward championship races. Combination of medal-deciding and semifinal races carries high programmatic weight despite early start time.

experience.explanation: Early-morning session (8:00 AM–10:10 AM) at Marine Stadium loses afternoon prime-time energy and draw. Cool coastal air and soft light appeal to dedicated fans, but compact 2-hour window and sunrise timing limit the casual spectator experience.

starPower.explanation: Karolien Florijn (Paris gold, women's single sculls) competes in semis determining her medal-final field. Men's quad sculls and fours A finals feature elite international crews; women's quad heats introduce additional top rowers. High concentration of world-class competitors.

uniqueness.explanation: Traditional boat classes (single sculls, quads, fours) in standard semifinal and final rounds. Early-morning timing is unusual but not iconic; the session follows the established competition arc without a singular defining moment.

demand.explanation: Price ceiling $229.46 with gold medals present, but 8 a.m. start time suppresses demand compared to afternoon medal sessions. Games-presale scarcity maintains interest, but morning timing makes tickets easier to secure than evening finals.

scorecard.overall: Men's quad sculls and fours crown champions while Florijn and Twigg race semis toward their own medals, but an 8 a.m. start time deflates the experience. Gold medals and star power merit attendance for dedicated rowing fans, but early timing limits broader appeal.

significance.explanation: Women's quad sculls and fours award all medals in A and B finals. Men's eights heats and men's single sculls semis add advancement stakes. Women's boat completion and eights debut combine for high programmatic significance.

experience.explanation: Mid-morning session (9:00 AM–11:00 AM) at Marine Stadium with reliable conditions and growing foot traffic. Eights—the sport's largest boats—deliver spectacular synchronized power; medal ceremonies add emotional focal points between rounds.

starPower.explanation: Oliver Zeidler races men's single sculls semis; women's quad finals feature elite international crews. Men's eights heats introduce eight rowers plus coxswain in one of rowing's most celebrated boat classes, drawing top programs worldwide.

uniqueness.explanation: Traditional boat classes in standard medal and preliminary rounds. Eights' debut is significant to the sport, but they're an established boat class, not a new or returning discipline. One of several sessions progressing the rowing schedule.

demand.explanation: Price ceiling $229.46 with women's gold medals, but 9 a.m. start time slightly suppresses demand versus afternoon slots. Growing morning crowds and eights spectacle maintain solid interest; easier tickets than evening finals but still Games-presale competitive.

scorecard.overall: Women's quad sculls and fours medal while men's eights—the sport's flagship boats—make their first appearance, and Zeidler pushes toward his championship race. Mid-morning timing and medal ceremonies provide engaging viewing, though traditional format lacks peak-time intensity.

significance.explanation: Women's single sculls A, B, C, D finals crown the sport's most prestigious individual title. Men's eights A, B finals award two major medals. Two marquee races (women's single gold and men's eights gold) make this the program's highest-stakes session to date.

experience.explanation: Late-morning session (10:00 AM–11:30 AM) at Marine Stadium with solid light and committed crowds gathering for the women's single sculls championship. One-on-one racing and eights' power create compelling finish-line theater; medal ceremonies cap the emotional arc.

starPower.explanation: Karolien Florijn (Paris gold, women's single sculls) races her championship final—the marquee individual title. Men's eights finals feature the world's best eight-person crews from elite rowing nations. Both A finals bring household names and world champions.

uniqueness.explanation: Women's single sculls championship is a singular, high-prestige moment on the rowing calendar. Eights are an established boat class, but the pairing of women's individual gold with men's eights medals creates a distinctive session combining the sport's most prestigious individual race with its most powerful boat class.

demand.explanation: Price ceiling $229.46; women's single sculls championship and men's eights medals drive high presale demand. Second-to-last rowing day with marquee races and strong star power—competitively sought, though demand peaks at the final session.

scorecard.overall: Florijn's defense of the women's single sculls title takes center stage alongside men's eights gold, making this the program's highest-stakes session before the closing day. Late-morning timing and historic venue deliver championship atmosphere, though not the absolute scarcity of the final session.

significance.explanation: Men's single sculls A, B, C, D finals award the last gold medal of classic rowing; women's eights A, B finals crown the program's final team title. Two marquee championship races close the traditional rowing program at Marine Stadium.

experience.explanation: Late-morning session (10:30 AM–11:50 AM) at Marine Stadium with clear light and energized crowds gathering for the program's conclusion. Final medal ceremonies and closing celebration of the historic venue amplify emotional weight; intimate timing before coastal rowing begins.

starPower.explanation: Oliver Zeidler (Paris gold, men's single sculls) races his championship final—one of rowing's most prestigious individual titles. Women's eights bring elite international crews; defending and emerging champions from top rowing programs compete for final medals.

uniqueness.explanation: Final day of classic rowing at the historic 1932 Marine Stadium venue creates a singular, once-in-a-Games moment. Men's single sculls championship mirrors the women's race but closes the traditional program; the pairing of individual and team gold in the program's epilogue is a distinctive historic occasion.

demand.explanation: Price ceiling $229.46; final rowing day at Marine Stadium with two marquee championship races and closing ceremonies. Scarcity of the program's conclusion, Zeidler's championship race, and closure of the historic venue combine to drive presale demand to its peak for rowing.

scorecard.overall: Zeidler's men's single sculls championship and women's eights gold conclude classic rowing at the iconic 1932 Marine Stadium, creating the program's final marquee moment. Late-morning timing and closing-day finality make this the most sought-after rowing session, though not a cross-sport blockbuster like basketball or track finals.

significance.explanation: Pool play determines quarterfinalist seeding but carries no medals. Critical for advancement, yet group-stage results only unlock knockout stakes, not decide them.

experience.explanation: Morning start at 10 AM in open-air Carson Stadium with steep seating creates intimate atmosphere for six rapid 14-minute matches, though daytime temperatures and early hour limit prime-time energy.

starPower.explanation: Paris 2024 medalists (New Zealand, Canada, Australia) expected across the six matches. World-class athletes certainly present, but pool play lacks the singular superstar focus of knockout rounds.

uniqueness.explanation: First women's session of the tournament, but opening pool play is routine in format and structure. The novelty is the Games start, not anything exceptional about this specific round.

demand.explanation: Low price ceiling ($28–$254) and morning time slot make this more accessible than medal rounds. Games scarcity prevents low demand, but presale tracking suggests easier booking than evening sessions.

scorecard.overall: Women's pool play introduction to a fast-paced format at an intimate venue with three expected medal contenders. Morning timing and preliminary stakes keep this accessible and foundational rather than must-see.

significance.explanation: Late-round pool play where group standings remain fluid and several teams still fight for survival. Stakes matter for seeding, but medals remain distant for lower-ranked sides.

experience.explanation: Evening 4–7 PM slot brings bigger crowds and cooler air to Carson Stadium. Six consecutive 14-minute matches maintain nonstop action, and prime-time start lifts atmosphere above morning sessions.

starPower.explanation: Paris 2024 medalists (New Zealand, Canada, Australia) confirmed across pool assignments. World-level talent throughout, though pool play gives teams flexibility in lineup deployment.

uniqueness.explanation: Second identical pool play session; format and round are standard. Evening timing offers slight novelty versus morning RU701, but the session itself is structurally routine.

demand.explanation: Prime-time evening slot and marginally higher price floor ($28–$254) suggest better ticket velocity than morning pools. Still preliminary-round scarcity, but preferred timing drives modest demand bump.

scorecard.overall: Evening women's pool play with steady action and likely medal contenders. Prime-time crowd atmosphere and confirmed top-tier participation offset the preliminary-stage stakes.

significance.explanation: Final pool round locks quarterfinal seeding and eliminates four teams from medal contention. Desperation and tactical depth rise, but medals still hinge on outcomes two days hence.

starPower.explanation: New Zealand, Canada, and Australia (Paris 2024 medalists) likely secure, putting expected lineups on field. Final-round stakes may prompt top players to deliver peak performances.

scorecard.overall: Final women's pool play where seeding and eliminations crystallize. Mounting stakes and confirmed top-tier talent elevate intensity, but daytime and preliminary format keep it accessible rather than urgent.

starPower.explanation: New Zealand, Canada, and Australia (Paris 2024 podium) projected to face off in quarterfinal matchups. Top-tier talent and likely medal-contender face-offs ensure global household names in the mix.

demand.explanation: Price floor jumps to $43.41 and quarterfinals carry knockout scarcity. Paris medalist rematches and single-elimination format drive higher demand versus pool play, though not yet medal-round tier.

scorecard.overall: Women's quarterfinals with expected clashes of Paris medalists and true single-elimination stakes. Afternoon timing, higher price point, and knockout intensity mark a step up, though not yet medal-stage fever.

significance.explanation: Semifinals are the final hurdle before medals. Losers face bronze; winners claim gold. Two rounds from podium, and QF winners advance. Medal implications crystallize here.

experience.explanation: Afternoon 1–4 PM start on medal day after rest allows buildup anticipation. Carson Stadium fills with heightened energy as fans await evening championship. Classification and placement matches maintain nonstop rhythm before medal rounds.

starPower.explanation: New Zealand defending Paris 2024 gold vs. Canada or Australia in semifinal rematches. World-class medal contenders guaranteed. Potential podium previews elevate global recognition.

scorecard.overall: Women's semifinals where Paris medalists likely clash toward podium finishes. Medal-day timing and confirmed top-tier participation create peak tournament energy, though gold/bronze medals decide rank.

significance.explanation: Gold and bronze medals are awarded. This session crowns the women's champion and defines final podium. No higher stakes exist in any Games event.

experience.explanation: Evening 7–9:30 PM night match under Carson Stadium lights. Peak atmosphere with crowds amped, medals on the line, and celebration meters from intimate stands. Close-proximity venue amplifies every roar.

starPower.explanation: New Zealand defending gold vs. medal contenders Canada and Australia. If all three reach podium, it's a marquee medal clash featuring globally-recognized rugby sevens champions.

uniqueness.explanation: Women's gold-medal final at an iconic venue (Carson Stadium, home of LA Galaxy). Once-per-Games session; the highest podium finish for women's rugby sevens in 2028.

scorecard.overall: Women's gold and bronze medals in prime-time evening rugby. World-class medal contenders, night-match theater, and once-per-Games championship status make this the flagship women's session.

demand.explanation: Low price ceiling ($28–$254) and morning slot make this accessible. Pool-play preliminaries and early timing suggest easier booking than evening or knockout sessions.

scorecard.overall: Men's pool play introduction with expected Paris medalists and fast-paced sevens format. Morning timing and preliminary stakes keep this foundational rather than essential viewing.

scorecard.overall: Evening men's pool play with steady action and likely medal contenders on field. Prime-time timing and confirmed top-tier participation offset preliminary-stage stakes.

experience.explanation: Morning 10 AM–1 PM session at Carson Stadium maintains close proximity and steep seating. Final-round intensity drives higher energy than opening pools, but daytime timing limits prime-time peak.

starPower.explanation: France, Argentina, and New Zealand (Paris 2024 medalists) likely deploy peak lineups to secure quarterfinal positions. Final-round stakes may prompt top-tier talent to deliver signature performances.

uniqueness.explanation: Third pool play session in identical format. Pool finales are standard tournament structure; Carson Stadium repetition across all men's events is expected.

demand.explanation: Last preliminary-round pricing ($28–$254) before quarterfinal bumps. Morning slot and pool-play format limit demand, though tournament-arc momentum adds minor boost versus earlier pools.

scorecard.overall: Final men's pool play where quarterfinal seeding and eliminations lock in. Mounting stakes and confirmed top-tier talent elevate intensity, but daytime and preliminary format keep this accessible.

significance.explanation: Opening day of kite qualification series carries minimal medal stakes—points are earned across three days, and finalists haven't been decided. World-class athletes competing for advancement, but preliminary round lacks the weight of medal-deciding rounds.

experience.explanation: Belmont Shore's sandy beach setup with ocean views offers solid spectator access to fast-paced foiling action at 30+ knots. Afternoon wind window is ideal for conditions, though the extended six-hour format and preliminary round energy fall short of finals intensity.

starPower.explanation: Field includes Paris 2024 medalists Valentin Bontus and Lauriane Nolot, but qualification preliminaries don't guarantee top names appear in every heat. Every competitor is a world-class athlete, though not all carry global household recognition.

uniqueness.explanation: Kite's return to the Games is notable, but this is one of three identical preliminary days—each session repeats the same format and racing structure. The novelty of the sport at Belmont Shore is shared across the qualification series.

demand.explanation: Entry-level $28 price point and preliminary round status suggest easier access than medal rounds. Niche sport among general audiences, though dedicated sailing fans and kite enthusiasts will seek out the opening.

scorecard.overall: Opening day of Formula Kite qualifiers at Belmont Shore brings the speed and spectacle of foiling boards to the Pacific shore. Preliminary round and repetitive format limit stakes and uniqueness, but accessible pricing and a live Games experience hold appeal for dedicated water sports fans.

significance.explanation: Windsurfing debuts day one of qualifiers alongside kite day two—both sessions are preliminary rounds building points toward future advancement. Medal positions remain wide open; no outcomes here lock in podium finishes.

experience.explanation: Double-header format with two foiling disciplines racing simultaneously offers variety and continuous action across the six-hour window. Belmont Shore's beachside vantage point lets spectators track both events, though afternoon preliminary sessions lack the concentrated drama of finals.

starPower.explanation: Paris 2024 gold medalists Luuc van Opzeeland and Sharon Kantor are likely competing, but preliminary scheduling doesn't guarantee all top names appear in every heat. Field depth is high; household recognition is limited.

uniqueness.explanation: Windsurfing's format—multi-day qualification series leading to finals—mirrors established Games sailing structure. Belmont Shore as the venue is new to Games hosting, but the double-header session type (combining two events) is a practical scheduling decision, not a singular occasion.

demand.explanation: Lowest price point at $28 and preliminary round status indicate more relaxed ticket competition than medal rounds. Niche appeal keeps demand softer, though the novelty of two board events in one afternoon may attract casual spectators.

scorecard.overall: Windsurfing and kite racing share a long six-hour preliminary session at Belmont Shore, offering variety but not medal stakes. Dual-event format adds visual interest; preliminary round and low price reflect softer demand and earlier positioning in the qualification arc.

significance.explanation: Windsurfing day two and kite day three (final qualifier) are the last chance to accumulate preliminary points, but advancement is not decided until final heats conclude. Midweek session remains in qualification phase without medal-deciding outcomes.

experience.explanation: Continuous dual-event action on the Pacific with shifting wind conditions and rotating heats creates dynamic spectating. Belmont Shore's close beach viewing and Southern California coastal backdrop frame afternoon racing, though preliminary round energy trails medal-round intensity.

starPower.explanation: Paris 2024 medalists are active in rankings and likely competing across both preliminary days, though qualification heats don't concentrate top talent in single moments. Mix of world champions and rising challengers in the field.

uniqueness.explanation: Third day of kite qualifiers and second day of windsurfing qualifiers follow the same multi-day format as earlier sessions. Midweek scheduling and dual-event pairing are functional choices, not once-in-a-lifetime combinations unique to this moment.

demand.explanation: Same $28 price tier as earlier preliminary sessions and midweek timing (Tuesday) suggest softer demand than finals. Niche sailing audience and preliminary round status keep ticket competition easier relative to medal-deciding rounds.

scorecard.overall: Kite qualifiers reach their final preliminary day while windsurfing builds momentum through day two, both on the Pacific coast. Routine preliminary format and midweek slot limit uniqueness and demand, though dual-event action and accessible pricing hold appeal for dedicated fans.

significance.explanation: Kite finals award the first sailing medals of the 2028 Games across men's and women's events. Compressed two-race format means any mistake costs podium position—peak stakes despite simultaneous windsurfing qualifiers still in progress.

experience.explanation: Kite finals deliver concentrated tactical drama with top 10 competitors in head-to-head medal races at 30+ knots. Six-hour window accommodates Pacific afternoon breezes, and Belmont Shore's sandy beach setup offers close ocean-view access to high-speed racing.

starPower.explanation: Paris 2024 gold medalist Lauriane Nolot and silver medalist Toni Vodišek are defending their positions in finals. Top 10 field includes world-ranked athletes competing directly for medals, elevating global recognition in the moment.

uniqueness.explanation: Kite medal finals at Belmont Shore represent the first climbing of the podium in the sport at the 2028 Games—a debut moment for this returning discipline. Medal-round specificity and kite's limited Games history create distinctness, though the venue and format follow Paris precedent.

demand.explanation: Price jump to $104.19 from $28 prelims signals premium positioning. Kite medal rounds draw core sailing enthusiasts and water sports fans seeking the climactic moment; Games scarcity and medal stakes drive higher ticket competition than earlier sessions.

scorecard.overall: Formula Kite medals are crowned as the top 10 race twice for gold on the Pacific, deciding the first sailing podium of the 2028 Games. Medal stakes and prize field elevate demand and significance; Belmont Shore's beach vantage point frames fast-paced foiling action in prime afternoon conditions.

significance.explanation: Men's and women's windsurfing finals award two gold medals across the iQFoil class. Compressed two-race format gives final competitors one last chance to swing the competition, deciding podium positions after three days of qualifiers.

experience.explanation: Windsurfing medal races concentrate high-skill tactical action in a three-hour window on the Pacific. Belmont Shore's close beach viewing captures foiling boards at speed; Thursday afternoon wind conditions are prime for coastal racing, though shorter duration limits spectator flexibility vs. six-hour sessions.

starPower.explanation: Paris 2024 gold medalists Sharon Kantor and Luuc van Opzeeland are likely contenders, with top 10 qualifiers including world-ranked iQFoil specialists. Finals concentrate the most accomplished athletes in direct medal competition.

uniqueness.explanation: Windsurfing medal finals at Belmont Shore mark the closing of board events and first windsurfing medals of the 2028 Games. iQFoil format mirrors Paris, but this specific session decides the top podium of the class—a distinct and climactic moment for the discipline.

demand.explanation: Premium $104.19 price and medal-round status place this among the most sought-after sailing sessions. Final day of board events and dual-gender medal races draw dedicated sailing followers and casual Games fans alike; limited supply of final-round sessions amplifies ticket competition.

scorecard.overall: Windsurfing crowns its champions as men's and women's iQFoil medals are decided in back-to-back races on the Pacific. Medal stakes, proven world-class contenders, and closing-act positioning drive strong demand; compressed three-hour format concentrates the drama.

significance.explanation: First qualification day opens the sailing program with no medals on the line. Dinghies are core events, but Day 1 prelims determine only seeding within the qualification series — real stakes, but furthest from medal moments.

experience.explanation: Six-hour afternoon block offers continuous action across open water with visible sail traffic. Harbor-side venue provides decent sightlines, though industrial backdrop and afternoon timing lack the energy of evening prime slots. Still, the marathon format means constant racing to watch.

starPower.explanation: World-class single-handed sailors are competing, but most are not globally recognizable names outside sailing circles. Top competitors will be present; the sport's dinghy specialists lack the crossover fame of marquee sports.

uniqueness.explanation: Standard opening qualification round of an established sailing format. Port of Los Angeles is historic for the 1932 Games but hosting the same dinghy series as many other venues — this specific session is one of two preliminary dinghy days with no unusual distinguishing factor.

demand.explanation: Lowest ticket price ($28) and earliest in the sailing schedule signals softer demand relative to finals. Completionists and dedicated sailing fans will seek this; general public more likely waits for medal races. Games scarcity sets a floor, but this is an easier ticket than closing days.

scorecard.overall: Opening day of Men's and Women's Dinghy qualification: continuous small-boat racing in the Port of Los Angeles with no medals at stake yet. Accessible price and pure sailing action, but lacks the drama of finals or star-name draw that pulls casual fans.

significance.explanation: Three classes race but all in qualification rounds — mixed dinghy and multihull open their series, while solo dinghies push into Day 2. No medals decided; the session sets standings for medal qualification.

experience.explanation: Packed six-hour afternoon window with six overlapping racecourses creates visual spectacle of hundreds of sails. Multihulls foil racing adds dynamic speed element alongside traditional dinghy tactics. Daytime slot and busy harbor action compensate for lack of evening atmosphere.

starPower.explanation: Roster includes defending Paris 2024 medalists from Spain, New Zealand, and the USA (projected to return in skiff finals, not yet visible here). Day 2 of qualification draws elite fleets, but individual crew names remain niche except within sailing community.

uniqueness.explanation: Three-class slate is busier than Day 1, but still a standard qualification day in an established sport at a conventional venue. The novelty of mixed multihull (Nacra 17) foiling is countered by routine dinghy racing — no medal drama or iconic pairing.

demand.explanation: Same $28 price point as SAL06 and comparable preliminary stakes. Slightly busier field might appeal to sailing enthusiasts interested in mixed-team racing, but demand remains softer than medal sessions.

scorecard.overall: Midweek three-class qualifier with mixed dinghies and multihulls opening their campaigns alongside solo dinghy Day 2. High sail-count spectacle and foiling catamarans add visual interest, but qualifying-round stakes and daytime slot keep it below premium demand.

significance.explanation: Four classes in qualification, including skiff debut (49er and 49erFX). Skiffs are high-profile within sailing, but Day 1 qualification carries softer medal stakes than later rounds. Dinghy and multihull classes continue series consistency-building.

experience.explanation: Most crowded day of the week with six racecourses and peak visual activity — hundreds of sails and rapid-fire announcer updates create genuine event energy. Skiff racing's acrobatic trapeze and speed add spectacle beyond traditional dinghies. Afternoon conditions and continuous six-hour window maintain engagement.

starPower.explanation: Projected Paris 2024 medalists from Spain, New Zealand, and USA are entering the skiff field. Defending gold crew from Spain and silver crew from New Zealand represent genuine international star power at Games level, though mostly unknown outside sailing. First appearance of these crews in 2028.

uniqueness.explanation: Skiff debut adds novelty, but it remains a qualification day in the broader series — no medals, no iconic venue pairing that elevates this session beyond standard Games sailing preliminaries. The busiest day has spectacle but not singular scarcity.

demand.explanation: Same $28 price and qualifying-round stakes as earlier days, but skiff presence and confirmed Paris 2024 medalist crews may draw stronger interest from sailing enthusiasts. Still preliminary; main demand spike arrives with medal races.

scorecard.overall: The week's most action-packed qualifying day as skiffs enter and six overlapping classes fill the harbor. Defending medal crews debut in high-performance racing, and acrobatic boat-handling creates spectacle — but it remains a preliminary round without medals on the line.

significance.explanation: First two sailing medals are decided in Men's and Women's Dinghy finals (double-point medal races). Dinghy is a core event; these are the opening gold, silver, and bronze moments of the 2028 sailing program. Medal-deciding stakes elevate the session substantially.

experience.explanation: Medal races with compact ten-boat fields create intense tactical battles. Afternoon prime wind timing and immediate dockside medal ceremonies afterward add ceremony and closure. Tighter field than qualification days allows spectators clearer view of decisive moments and winners.

starPower.explanation: Top single-handed sailors from around the world compete for gold, including defending and aspiring champions. Names remain niche outside sailing circles, but these are world-level elite competitors fighting for podiums at the Games.

uniqueness.explanation: First sailing medals of 2028 carry historical weight as the opening podiums of the regatta. Dinghy medal race is established format, but this specific session is the gateway moment that launches the sailing program into medal territory. Iconic timing within the week.

demand.explanation: Price jumps to $104.19 — roughly 3.7x SAL06 — signaling medal-round premium. Sailing fans and Games completionists prioritize medal sessions. Demand is strong though not at peak scarcity levels of marquee sports finals.

scorecard.overall: Opening medal races for Men's and Women's Dinghy crown the first sailing gold medalists of 2028. Higher price and decisive stakes distinguish this from qualifiers, though single-handed boat racing and niche athlete recognition limit demand relative to mainstream sports.

significance.explanation: Two more sailing medals: Mixed Dinghy (470) and Mixed Multihull (Nacra 17) hold medal races with double-point finals determining gold, silver, bronze. Back-to-back medal moments with different disciplines (teamwork dinghies and foiling multihulls) expand the medal portfolio. Skiff qualification Day 3 sets the stage for Friday's skiff finals.

experience.explanation: Two distinct medal-race styles in one session: synchronized 470 crew tactics contrasted with high-speed Nacra 17 foiling creates dynamic spectacle. Afternoon sun and building breeze promise ideal conditions. Immediate dockside celebrations after each final and continuous action across the full window maintain energy.

starPower.explanation: Mixed-team events feature defending Paris 2024 medalists competing. Elite crew pairings and experienced mixed-doubles sailors are present, though still not household names outside sailing. Games-level competition and confirmed medal contenders from prior editions.

uniqueness.explanation: Nacra 17 foiling multihulls are visually striking and relatively newer to the Games format. Double medal-race day accelerates drama compared to single-class sessions. Not once-in-a-lifetime but distinctive within the week — two classes crowning medalists simultaneously creates concentrated milestone moment.

demand.explanation: Same $104.19 price tier as SAL09. Two medal races and unusual multihull foiling spectacle attract sailing enthusiasts. Mixed-team events draw partners and broader social attendance. Still below peak scarcity of absolute-final sessions, but clearly premium compared to qualifiers.

scorecard.overall: Two medal races in mixed disciplines: nimble 470 teamwork and speed-foiling Nacra 17 catamarans. Synchronized crew racing and striking multihull dynamics elevate the spectacle; medal stakes and confirmed Paris medalists drive demand above qualifier pricing.

significance.explanation: Final medal races of the 2028 sailing program: Men's and Women's Skiff (49er / 49erFX) are the last two medals awarded across all sailing classes. Skiff is the pinnacle discipline within sailing — most athletic and technically demanding. Closing-day medal significance carries weight.

experience.explanation: Skiff racing offers peak spectacle: crews trapeze-hiking at extreme angles, spinnaker work at 20-knot speeds, and acrobatic boat handling create visceral live-sport drama. Doubled points in medal races guarantee maximum tactical intensity. Three-hour afternoon window with building wind and immediate medal ceremonies.

starPower.explanation: Spain's Paris 2024 gold crew, New Zealand's silver medalists, and USA's bronze team are all projected to compete in Men's 49er if they qualify. These are genuine returning champions with global Games profile within sailing. Women's 49erFX features equivalent defending medalists.

uniqueness.explanation: Last medals of the sailing program at the most athletically demanding event in the regatta. Skiff finals represent the culmination moment — not a routine session but the capstone. Port of Los Angeles as the closing venue for skiff is appropriately iconic within the 2028 sailing arc.

demand.explanation: Premium $104.19 ticket price is standard for finals, but final-day sailing closing and returning Paris 2024 medal crews create scarcity. Sailing community and Games completionists prioritize this session. Demand is strong, though not at absolute peak of opening-ceremony or marquee-sport-final levels.

scorecard.overall: The 2028 sailing program's final medals: Men's and Women's Skiff in back-to-back medal races. Defending Paris 2024 medalists and acrobatic high-speed boat-handling create the most dynamic racing of the week, and closing-day significance drives premium demand among sailing enthusiasts.

significance.explanation: Women's and men's 10m Air Rifle gold medals on the Games' opening shooting day sets high stakes—two medals awarded in a single session that launches the rifle and pistol program. First-day prestige and medal finality drive significance.

experience.explanation: Afternoon-to-evening slot (1:45 PM–9:00 PM) at a purpose-built indoor range with 13,500 tiered seats overlooking firing lines. The controlled environment and audible precision (pellet clicks, breath control) create intimate spectator experience unusual for competitive shooting.

starPower.explanation: 10m Air Rifle attracts world-class shooters and past medalists, though few command household-name recognition outside shooting circles. Paris 2024 medalists and national champions will compete, but stardom tilts toward niche enthusiasts rather than global celebrities.

uniqueness.explanation: Opening-day shooting carries prestige, but 10m Air Rifle qualification-to-final format is standard across Games cycles. The medal-and-done structure and first-day timing prevent this from being a once-in-a-lifetime pairing; it's the canonical rifle session replicated every four years.

demand.explanation: Games scarcity and opening-day prestige drive interest, but shooting lacks the marquee sport pulling power of basketball or athletics. Expect moderate-to-strong demand among Games completists and shooting enthusiasts, with easier general-public access than track or court finals.

scorecard.overall: The 2028 Games' first shooting medals in an indoor waterfront venue with direct qualification-to-finals action. Opening-day prestige and medalist finality anchor the session, though the sport's niche profile keeps it below marquee-tier demand.

significance.explanation: Two gold medals (men's and women's 10m Air Pistol) awarded in a single evening session—medal finality plus the technical distinction of one-handed shooting at pistol distance. Second consecutive medal-awarding day sustains opening-week momentum.

experience.explanation: Evening prime-time window (1:45 PM–9:00 PM) in Long Beach's indoor arena with electronic scoring displays and broadcast lighting. The one-handed pistol stance offers visual contrast to rifle; proximity to crowd amplifies the sport's quiet intensity.

starPower.explanation: Air pistol fields include past medalists and national champions, but household recognition remains limited to dedicated shooting audiences. The discipline demands spectacular precision rather than explosive athleticism, limiting crossover appeal.

uniqueness.explanation: Second consecutive day of air shooting finals follows the established format. While air pistol's one-handed mechanics differentiate it technically from air rifle, the qualification-to-final session structure and venue remain routine for a 15-event program.

demand.explanation: Back-to-back medal days and evening timing boost attractiveness. Games presale momentum from day one carries forward, but shooting's minority-sport status keeps demand below marquee finals. Expect solid availability compared to basketball or track.

scorecard.overall: Second shooting gold-medal session with the tactical contrast of one-handed pistol competition at evening prime time. Sustained opening-week energy and clear medal stakes, moderated by the sport's specialized audience.

significance.explanation: Opening qualification round with no medal stakes—athletes compete for advancement only. Meaningful for competitors but not for medal outcomes.

experience.explanation: Morning start at 9:30 AM limits prime-time energy; open-air range offers good sightlines and clear sound, but daytime spectating lacks the intensity of evening finals.

starPower.explanation: World-class shooters compete in qualification, but top athletes don't necessarily carry household recognition outside shooting circles. Every competitor is a national champion-level athlete.

uniqueness.explanation: Standard qualification day in an established discipline at a functional venue. First day of skeet adds minor novelty, but format mirrors Paris 2024 closely.

demand.explanation: Early prelim of a niche sport with modest price ceiling ($28–$104). Easier ticket access than medal rounds, though Games scarcity keeps demand above truly routine sporting events.

scorecard.overall: Opening skeet qualification at Whittier Narrows—world-class athletes in action, but morning timing and qualification stakes make this a solid entry point rather than a must-see session. Best for genuine shooting enthusiasts or completists.

significance.explanation: Four medals awarded in a single afternoon-evening session (both rifle and pistol mixed teams, men's and women's or combined). The unified four-team final format concentrates medal intensity and creates a rare multi-discipline medal stage within one competition.

experience.explanation: Monday afternoon-to-evening (3:00 PM–9:00 PM) with variety unmatched elsewhere in shooting—rifle qualification, rifle final, pistol qualification, pistol final back-to-back. Spectators witness four distinct technical formats and four medal ceremonies within hours at a configured indoor range.

starPower.explanation: Mixed team pairings feature Paris 2024 medalists and national champions competing together. Team formats occasionally elevate lesser-known athletes into medal contention through partnership, broadening the field of potential winners versus individual events.

uniqueness.explanation: The unified four-team final format (versus split gold/bronze matches) is a 2028 change that adds structural novelty. Pairing two disciplines and both genders in a single session is operationally rare and creates a compressed, multi-medal stage unlikely to occur elsewhere in the shooting program.

demand.explanation: Four medals and the mixed-team format novelty boost appeal. Early-week timing and concentrated medal density attract completists and casual Games fans seeking efficient access to multiple medal rounds. Expect strong demand relative to single-discipline sessions.

scorecard.overall: A uniquely compressed session awarding four shooting medals across rifle and pistol mixed teams with a 2028 format change. Afternoon-to-evening timing and structural variety position it as the shooting program's most efficient medal-watching experience.

significance.explanation: Two medal finals (men's and women's skeet) decided in a single session. Medal stakes combined with the expanded eight-finalist format from 2026 create higher-drama elimination rounds.

experience.explanation: All-day event spans morning qualifiers into afternoon finals under Southern California sun; open-air setting with close viewing works well for clay shooting, but daytime doesn't deliver evening prime-time energy.

starPower.explanation: Finalists represent the world's elite skeet shooters competing for gold, but shotgun lacks global celebrity names. Strong talent, modest household recognition outside the sport.

uniqueness.explanation: Medal round with updated eight-finalist format differs from Paris 2024, offering a structural change to the competition. Finals at Whittier Narrows carry weight, but the session type itself is standard.

demand.explanation: Medal finals drive interest and price ceiling ($104); stronger ticket demand than qualifications but softer than marquee sports. Shooting fans prioritize this heavily.

scorecard.overall: Skeet medal finals with the new eight-shooter format—real stakes and a structural update from Paris, but daytime execution and modest global star names prevent this from reaching top-tier demand. Core appeal to shooting followers.

significance.explanation: Men's 50m Rifle 3 Positions gold medal with the 2028 format change limiting finals to standing-position-only shooting. The standing-only final heightens precision stakes by removing the endurance element that qualification rewards.

experience.explanation: Morning session (9:30 AM–2:00 PM) with natural light from convention center windows and the full 50-meter range visible from upper tiers. Early timing reduces prime-time energy, but the extended range and eagle's-eye viewing angle provide spatial spectacle unavailable in shorter-distance events.

starPower.explanation: 50m Rifle 3 Positions attracts elite world-class competitors and past medalists, especially shooters known for standing-position excellence. Paris 2024 medalists and strong contenders will be present, though the discipline remains specialist-focused.

uniqueness.explanation: The standing-position-only final format is a 2028 change adding novelty, but 50m Rifle 3 Positions itself is an established event held at every Games. The format shift moderates uniqueness—it's a meaningful refinement rather than a debut or radical restructuring.

demand.explanation: Morning timing and the technical specificity of 50m rifle limit mainstream appeal. Stronger demand among shooting enthusiasts and completists; easier general-public ticket access than evening finals. Games presale baseline keeps it above casual event level, but softer than marquee sessions.

scorecard.overall: Men's 50m Rifle 3 Positions gold on a morning with the 2028 standing-only final format change. Format novelty and full-range spectator sightlines anchor the session, though morning timing and specialized appeal moderate broad demand.

significance.explanation: Women's 50m Rifle 3 Positions gold medal with standing-only final format. Mirrors men's session structure and 2028 format change, maintaining equal medal weight and technical significance.

experience.explanation: Thursday morning (9:30 AM–2:00 PM) with natural convention center lighting and full 50-meter range visibility. Daytime timing reduces energy compared to evening sessions, but the extended range and tiered sightlines offer spatial advantage. Waterfront location adds post-session appeal.

starPower.explanation: Women's 50m Rifle 3 Positions features Paris 2024 medalists and world-level competitors. The discipline attracts elite shooters, though global household recognition remains limited to shooting-focused audiences.

uniqueness.explanation: Standing-position-only final is a 2028 format change mirroring the men's event. The women's version maintains established structure while incorporating the same format novelty; neither debut nor radical restructuring prevents it from feeling like one of multiple similar sessions across the program.

demand.explanation: Morning timing and technical specialization soften demand relative to evening finals. Women's shooting attracts dedicated audience support, but the broader casual Games viewer tilts toward marquee sports. Easier general-public access than marquee medal finals.

scorecard.overall: Women's 50m Rifle 3 Positions gold medal in the morning with the 2028 standing-final format change. Structural novelty and full-range venue advantage, offset by daytime timing and specialized appeal limiting mainstream demand.

significance.explanation: First day of trap qualification with no medal deciding—competitors vie for advancement to finals. Stakes exist for athletes but not yet for podium positioning.

experience.explanation: Morning start and preliminary format soften the spectator experience; Whittier Narrows' multiple trap fields offer decent viewing, but no evening atmosphere or medal pressure to drive excitement.

starPower.explanation: Elite trap shooters from around the world compete, but qualification round doesn't guarantee top-tier names in action. Roster is strong, recognition is niche.

uniqueness.explanation: Unchanged trap qualification format from Paris 2024 at a functional venue. No structural innovation or cultural resonance—textbook preliminary round.

demand.explanation: Early prelim with low price ceiling ($28–$104). Easier to obtain than medal rounds; Games scarcity keeps baseline demand, but this is the softest session in the shotgun program.

scorecard.overall: Trap qualification day one—world-class athletes in a standard preliminary format with no medal stakes yet. Daytime slot and qualification stakes position this as entry-level for casual Games attendees or shooting specialists.

significance.explanation: Women's 25m Pistol qualification precision stage—preliminary, non-medal competition determining advancement. Stakes exist for athletes, but medals are uncontested; this is mandatory preparation for qualification completion rather than medal-deciding action.

experience.explanation: Morning preliminary (9:30 AM–12:00 PM) with slow-fire precision format emphasizing technical control over dramatic action. Multiple relays and early timing reduce prime-time energy, though the accessible, methodical pace suits casual spectators learning the sport.

starPower.explanation: World-class shooters compete in qualification, including past medalists and national champions, but the preliminary round rarely highlights the biggest names. Star athletes are present but not yet separated from the field.

uniqueness.explanation: Qualification preliminaries are standard across all Games shooting programs. The precision stage format is established; while technically specific, the session itself is routine—one of multiple preliminary qualification rounds throughout the program with no special venue or format novelty.

demand.explanation: Morning preliminary timing and lack of medal stakes reduce competitive demand. Easier tickets than finals; mostly attracted to shooting enthusiasts and completists. Games baseline prevents true low demand, but this is the softest ticket among shooting sessions.

scorecard.overall: A morning women's 25m Pistol qualification preliminary emphasizing precision-stage technique over medal drama. Entry-level shooting experience for completists; accessible timing and technical clarity, limited by lack of medal stakes and high preliminary accessibility.

significance.explanation: Two gold-medal finals (men's and women's trap) decided with eight-finalist expanded format. Medal rounds with high elimination pressure across both disciplines.

experience.explanation: All-day format with afternoon finals in late-day light; open-air range setting is conducive to clay shooting. No evening prime-time atmosphere, and daytime heat trade against the visual drama of shadows and closing light.

starPower.explanation: Trap medal finalists are world-caliber elite shooters, but shotgun competitors remain largely unknown outside niche circles. Strong credentials, limited global brand recognition.

uniqueness.explanation: Medal finals with the new eight-competitor format from 2026 add structural differentiation from Paris 2024. Not a once-in-a-lifetime pairing, but a medal round with format innovation.

demand.explanation: Medal finals drive ticket demand and price ceiling ($104); stronger than qualifications but constrained by sport's smaller global following. Shooting enthusiasts will prioritize this.

scorecard.overall: Trap medal finals with expanded eight-shooter format—solid medal stakes and structural novelty, but daytime execution and shooting's niche profile limit top-tier prestige. Strong draw for the shooting community.

significance.explanation: Women's 25m Pistol gold medal awarded with the session combining qualification rapid-fire completion and medal finals. Two stages of qualification funneling to the top eight, then the afternoon final deciding medals—singular medal-awarding conclusion for the discipline.

experience.explanation: Sunday morning-to-afternoon (9:30 AM–2:00 PM) featuring the distinctive rapid-fire format's staccato rhythm—three-second target exposures and quick-succession shots creating pace absent from slower disciplines. Afternoon prime-time push and the mixed rapid/precision final format sustain spectator engagement.

starPower.explanation: The rapid-fire stage attracts specialized elite shooters known for reaction speed and precision under time pressure. Paris 2024 medalists are likely present; the discipline's technical difficulty concentrates world-class competitors even in qualification, elevating field prominence.

uniqueness.explanation: The 25m Pistol mixing rapid and precision series within a single final is noted as the only pistol event combining two timing formats. This technical distinction adds novelty, though qualification-to-final structure and the session itself remain standard across the program.

demand.explanation: Medal finality and the rapid-fire format's visual and auditory distinctiveness boost demand. Sunday timing and completion of women's pistol attract Games followers and shooting enthusiasts. Moderate-to-strong demand relative to other preliminaries, though still below marquee finals.

scorecard.overall: Women's 25m Pistol qualification completion and gold-medal final with distinctive rapid/precision mixed format. Morning-to-afternoon timing and the only pistol event blending two timing methods position it as women's pistol showcase; medal stakes and format novelty drive demand despite daytime slot.

significance.explanation: Mixed team trap medal final—one of only two mixed-gender shooting events at the Games. Medal gold decided with fresh integrated format replacing separate gold/bronze matches.

experience.explanation: Marathon same-day format (9:30 AM–4:45 PM) compresses qualification and finals without intermission or nighttime energy. Open-air range offers good sightlines but lacks evening atmosphere or focal build.

starPower.explanation: Pairs elite male and female shooters on national teams, but mixed-team format dilutes individual celebrity. Roster is strong, but pairing structure softens star appeal.

uniqueness.explanation: Only mixed-team shooting event on the 2028 program and the sole mixed-gender trap discipline. Updated integrated finals format (all qualifiers compete together) differs meaningfully from Paris 2024's separate matches.

demand.explanation: Medal final elevates demand, but niche sport, same-day format, and daytime execution keep ticket competition moderate. Smaller audience than individual finals; easier access than skeet/trap pair sessions.

scorecard.overall: Mixed team trap medal final with updated integrated format—distinctive as the Games' only mixed-gender shooting event, but a marathon daytime session and shotgun's niche status limit universal appeal. Notable for format innovation, not blockbuster prestige.

significance.explanation: Men's 25m Rapid Fire Pistol Stage 1—preliminary qualification where medals remain undecided. The expanded eight-finalist format increases importance of Stage 1 qualification scores, but no medals are awarded in this session.

experience.explanation: Monday afternoon-to-evening (2:30 PM–7:00 PM) capturing the rapid-fire format's staccato rhythm and explosive hand-eye coordination. Multiple relays through early evening and the distinctive four-second target sequences create spectacle absent from slow-fire events, offsetting the preliminary status.

starPower.explanation: Rapid-fire specialists and past medalists are competing, but qualification stages rarely separate top from middle-tier competitors clearly. World-class shooters are present throughout the field without yet emerging as finalists.

uniqueness.explanation: Stage 1 is a standard preliminary qualification format repeated across the shooting program. The expanded eight-finalist structure is a 2028 change, but Stage 1 itself remains routine—one of multiple preliminary sessions with no format novelty or venue distinction.

demand.explanation: Afternoon-to-evening timing and the rapid-fire format's distinctive action elevate appeal relative to slower preliminaries. However, non-medal status and qualification-stage stakes limit demand compared to finals. Accessible tickets; appeals primarily to shooting enthusiasts and progressive completists.

scorecard.overall: Men's 25m Rapid Fire Pistol Stage 1 qualification in afternoon-to-evening with the format's explosive staccato rhythm. Preliminary stakes without medal finality; improved experience over slow-fire sessions, moderated by lack of medal drama and Stage 1 routine structure.

significance.explanation: Men's 25m Rapid Fire Pistol final awarding the last shooting gold medal of the Games. Stage 2 completion plus the medal-deciding final concentrates the rifle and pistol program's closing stakes into a single session—maximum significance as program-closing gold.

experience.explanation: Tuesday afternoon-to-evening (2:30 PM–9:15 PM) with the rapid-fire format's explosive tempo and eight-finalist structure generating highest-energy pistol action. Expanding finalists to eight increases competitive drama; evening slot and the rapid-succession shooting (multiple four-second series) create peak spectacle before shooting concludes.

starPower.explanation: The final attracts Paris 2024 medalists, world-record holders, and elite rapid-fire specialists. The expanded eight-finalist format brings more competitive parity and star representation; the final stage separates and highlights the world's best rapid-fire shooters under maximum pressure.

uniqueness.explanation: The expanded eight-finalist format is a 2028 change adding competitive novelty. More importantly, this session closes the entire rifle and pistol program—a program-finale medal match with format-level distinction from Paris. Not iconic-venue or debut-sport uniqueness, but structurally rare as closing shooting gold.

demand.explanation: Program-closing gold medal, expanded finalist field, evening prime-time, and the rapid-fire format's visual excitement combine for strong demand. Shooting enthusiasts and Games completists prioritize this as the final opportunity for rifle/pistol medals. Expect near-full capacity and elevated pricing.

scorecard.overall: The Games' final shooting gold medal featuring men's 25m Rapid Fire Pistol with the expanded eight-finalist format and explosive four-second target sequences. Program-closing prestige, format novelty, and evening prime-time positioning anchor the strongest demand of non-opening shooting sessions.

significance.explanation: Preliminary round with qualification stakes for the finalists, but no medals decided. Advancement matters intensely for competitors; for spectators, the medal drama arrives two days later.

experience.explanation: Evening session (5pm start) at a purpose-built open-air venue with close sightlines to the course. Temporary Valley Complex 1 lacks the historic gravitas of permanent Games landmarks but delivers intimate, adrenaline-fueled action in a park setting.

starPower.explanation: Paris 2024 medalists Coco Yoshizawa (gold), Liz Akama (silver), and Rayssa Leal (bronze) are expected to compete, bringing internationally recognized names even in a preliminary round where top seeds typically advance.

uniqueness.explanation: First skateboarding event held in the San Fernando Valley at the 2028 Games, marking the valley's Games debut; however, women's street prelims are a standard format repeated across Games cycles, lacking the once-in-a-lifetime pairing of venue and stakes.

demand.explanation: Low price ceiling ($28–$527) and preliminary-round status mean easier ticket availability than medal rounds, though skateboarding's youth appeal and Games scarcity still drive solid presale interest.

scorecard.overall: Women's street kicks off skateboarding in the Valley with Paris medalists on the course and evening energy, but the qualification stakes and preliminary format mean it trails the finals in intrigue and demand.

significance.explanation: Men's street preliminary—competitors battle for finals spots under Games pressure, but medals remain 48+ hours away. Same advancement-or-elimination format as women's prelim, with no medal-round stakes.

experience.explanation: Morning session (10:30am) in full San Fernando Valley sun limits the prime-time energy that skateboarding thrives on in evening rounds. Temporary open-air venue works better with crowds and darkness; daytime prelim flattens atmosphere.

starPower.explanation: Yuto Horigome (Paris gold), Jagger Eaton (Paris silver), and Nyjah Huston (Paris bronze) are all high-profile names with major prestige and international recognition, elevating the field despite the preliminary format.

uniqueness.explanation: Men's street prelim is a standard qualifying round; the Valley venue marks the first San Fernando Valley skateboarding at the Games, but the session itself is a routine preliminary with no special contextual distinction.

demand.explanation: Morning time slot and preliminary status create the softest demand of the skateboarding batch. Low price ceiling ($28–$527) and lack of medal stakes mean wider ticket availability compared to evening finals.

scorecard.overall: Morning prelim with elite Paris medalists competing, but daytime scheduling and non-medal stakes undercut both the live experience and ticket desirability compared to evening finals.

significance.explanation: Gold-medal final with medals on the line and the sport's defining moment decided. Skateboarding's best-trick format delivers high-stakes drama; women's street is a marquee event within a returning sport.

experience.explanation: Evening prime-time slot (8:30pm) at a purpose-built temporary venue with close spectator sightlines to the course. Nighttime atmosphere and medal finals create electric energy; Valley Complex 1's design puts crowds feet from every trick attempt.

starPower.explanation: Paris 2024 gold medalist Coco Yoshizawa (14-year-old phenom), silver medalist Liz Akama, and bronze medalist Rayssa Leal are all globally recognized young skateboarding superstars with compelling storylines and international followings.

uniqueness.explanation: Women's street final is skateboarding's marquee moment at the 2028 Games, but street finals follow a known format from Paris; uniqueness is tempered by the sport's repetition across Games cycles, though the Valley's inaugural hosting adds modest novelty.

demand.explanation: Gold-medal final with evening prime time and highest price ceiling of the three sessions ($93–$682). Medal stakes, high star power, and optimal scheduling drive strong presale velocity; expect near-capacity demand.

scorecard.overall: Women's street medals decided under Valley lights with Paris medallists competing—the skateboarding final most likely to sell out. Evening timing and gold stakes outweigh the venue's temporary status.

significance.explanation: Gold-medal final in skateboarding with all 22 world-qualifying competitors on the line. Medals awarded across two runs and five tricks — full weight of the sport's marquee night.

significance.explanation: Round of 16 in single-elimination format carries real advancement stakes, but no medals are on the line. First qualifying round of squash's debut still matters for the athletes competing.
